{{Infobox religious building | name = Kenkun Shrine | image = Kagura-den at Kenkun Jinja Spring 2010.JPG | caption = The [[Kagura]]den at Kenkun shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|19|N|135|44|35|E|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Oda Nobunaga]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Meiji]] | established = 1869 | date_destroyed = | location = Kyōto-fu
Kyōto-shi
Kita-ku
Murasakino
Kitafunaoka-machi 49 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kenkun Shrine'''|建勲神社,|''Kenkun-jinja''}} also known as '''Takeisao Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. One of the four shrines that protect Kyoto in the four cardinal directions, it protects Kyoto from the north. [[Oda Nobunaga]], a ''[[daimyō]]'' and key figure in the unification of Japan during the late 16th century, is [[Imperial cult|deified]] and buried inside.{{Cite book| last=Dougill |first=John | title =Kyoto: a cultural history | publisher =Oxford University Press | year =2006 | pages =111 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=ggmbp2pv8toC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA111 | isbn =978-0-19-530138-0 }} ==Overview== [[zh:建勳神社]][[ja:建勲神社]] {{Otheruses|Kenkun Shrine in Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kenkun Shrine in Tendo, Yamagata|Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)|Kenkun Shrine in Tamba, Hyogo|Kenkun Shrine (Tamba)}} {{神社 |名称= Kenkun Shrine |画像=[[File:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |所在地=[[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], Murasakino Kitafunaokayama-cho 49 |緯度度=35 |緯度分=2 |緯度秒=19 |N(北緯)及びS(南緯)=N |経度度=135 |経度分=44 |経度秒=35 |E(東経)及びW(西経)=E |地図国コード=JP |祭神=[[Oda Nobunaga]] and [[Oda Nobutada]] |社格=Former [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]]
Former [[Bekkaku Shrine]] |創建=1870 (Meiji 3) |本殿= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |例祭=July 1 |地図=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. The former shrine rank was [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] and it was previously a [[Bekkaku Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is [[Oda Nobunaga]], with his son [[Oda Nobutada]] also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by [[Emperor Meiji]] to honor [[Oda Nobunaga]], who aimed for the unification of Japan during the [[Sengoku period]] and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[genbu]] area of the [[Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Bekkaku Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - Deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - Deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == Festivals == The Funaoka Grand Festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == Related Topics == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たけいさおしんしや}} [[Category:Kenkun Shrines|!]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Kyoto City]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[Category:History of Kita-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Oda Nobunaga|Shrines dedicated to Oda Nobunaga]] [[Category:Buildings completed in 1880]] [[Category:19th-century establishments in Japan]] ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[Four Symbols]] **[[Black Tortoise]] *[[Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1869]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in 1869]] [[Category:1869 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Former Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Dd]][[en:Kenkun Shrine]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Kenkun Shrine | image = Kagura-den at Kenkun Jinja Spring 2010.JPG | caption = The [[Kagura]]den at Kenkun shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|19|N|135|44|35|E|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Oda Nobunaga]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Meiji]] | established = 1869 | date_destroyed = | location = Kyōto-fu
Kyōto-shi
Kita-ku
Murasakino
Kitafunaoka-machi 49 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kenkun Shrine'''|建勲神社,|''Kenkun-jinja''}} also known as '''Takeisao Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. One of the four shrines that protect Kyoto in the four cardinal directions, it protects Kyoto from the north. [[Oda Nobunaga]], a ''[[daimyō]]'' and key figure in the unification of Japan during the late 16th century, is [[Imperial cult|deified]] and buried inside.{{Cite book| last=Dougill |first=John | title =Kyoto: a cultural history | publisher =Oxford University Press | year =2006 | pages =111 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=ggmbp2pv8toC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA111 | isbn =978-0-19-530138-0 }} ==Overview== [[zh:建勳神社]][[ja:建勲神社]] {{Otheruses|Kenkun Shrine in Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kenkun Shrine in Tendo, Yamagata|Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)|Kenkun Shrine in Tamba, Hyogo|Kenkun Shrine (Tamba)}} {{神社 |名称= Kenkun Shrine |画像=[[File:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |所在地=[[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], Murasakino Kitafunaokayama-cho 49 |緯度度=35 |緯度分=2 |緯度秒=19 |N(北緯)及びS(南緯)=N |経度度=135 |経度分=44 |経度秒=35 |E(東経)及びW(西経)=E |地図国コード=JP |祭神=[[Oda Nobunaga]] and [[Oda Nobutada]] |社格=Former [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]]
Former [[Bekkaku Shrine]] |創建=1870 (Meiji 3) |本殿= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |例祭=July 1 |地図=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. The former shrine rank was [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] and it was previously a [[Bekkaku Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is [[Oda Nobunaga]], with his son [[Oda Nobutada]] also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by [[Emperor Meiji]] to honor [[Oda Nobunaga]], who aimed for the unification of Japan during the [[Sengoku period]] and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[genbu]] area of the [[Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Bekkaku Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - Deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - Deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == Festivals == The Funaoka Grand Festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == Related Topics == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たけいさおしんしや}} [[Category:Kenkun Shrines|!]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Kyoto City]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[Category:History of Kita-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Oda Nobunaga|Shrines dedicated to Oda Nobunaga]] [[Category:Buildings completed in 1880]] [[Category:19th-century establishments in Japan]] ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[Four Symbols]] **[[Black Tortoise]] *[[Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1869]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in 1869]] [[Category:1869 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Former Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[en:Kenkun Shrine]]

{{Otheruses|京都市北区にある建勲神社|山形県天童市にある建勲神社|建勲神社 (天童市)|兵庫県丹波市にある建勲神社|建勲神社 (丹波市)}} {{神社 |名称=建勲神社 |画像=[[画像:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
拝殿
{{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-width=270|type=point}} |所在地=[[京都府]][[京都市]][[北区 (京都市)|北区]]紫野北舟岡町49 | 緯度度 = 35 | 緯度分 = 2 | 緯度秒 = 19 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 135 |経度分 = 44 | 経度秒 = 35 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP |祭神=[[織田信長]]公、[[織田信忠]]卿 |社格=旧[[別格官幣社]]
元・[[別表神社]] |創建=[[1870年]]（[[明治]]3年） |本殿= 一間社[[流造]] |例祭=7月1日 |地図=Japan Kyoto city }} '''建勲神社'''（たけいさおじんじゃ）は、[[京都市]][[北区 (京都市)|北区]]紫野北舟岡町にある[[神社]]。旧社格は[[別格官幣社]]で元・[[別表神社]]。[[船岡山]]の中腹にある。[[織田信長]]を主祭神とし、子の[[織田信忠]]を配祀する。正しくは「たけいさおじんじゃ」と読むが、一般には「けんくんじんじゃ」と呼ばれ、「建勲（けんくん）さん」と通称される同神社最寄りの京都市バスのバス停の「建勲神社前」は「けんくんじんじゃまえ」と読む。。旧称'''健織田社'''（たけしおりたのやしろ）、'''建勲社'''（たけいさおしゃ）。[[江戸時代]]後期から[[明治]]時代初期に流行した[[藩祖]]を祀った神社のひとつである。 == 歴史 == [[1869年]]（[[明治]]2年）[[11月8日 (旧暦)|11月8日]]、「日本が外国に侵略されなかったのは、天下統一をめざして日本を一つにまとめた信長のおかげ」として[http://zasshi.news.yahoo.co.jp/article?a=20141116-00000001-sasahi-peo　織田家第18代当主　織田家の400年の伝統を破り、息子の名に「信」をつけず〈週刊朝日 　2014年11月21日号〉]dot. 11月16日、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]を天下統一、朝儀復興などを進めた[[織田信長]]を賛えるための'''健織田社'''（たけしおりたのやしろ）の創建が[[明治天皇]]により決定された。翌[[1870年]]（明治3年）[[10月17日 (旧暦)|10月17日]]、「建勲」の神号を賜り、信長の子孫で[[天童藩]][[知藩事|知事]]・[[織田信敏]]の[[東京]]の邸内と[[織田氏|織田家]]旧領地の[[山形県]][[天童市]]に建勲社が造営された（天童市に造営された建勲社については[[建勲神社 (天童市)]]を参照のこと）。[[1875年]]（明治8年）4月24日に[[近代社格制度|別格官幣社]]に列格した。 現在地の[[船岡山]]は[[平安京]]の[[四神相応]]の[[玄武]]に位置し、完成しなかったとはいえ[[豊臣秀吉]]によって信長の廟所・[[天正寺 (京都市)|天正寺]]の境内地と定められた場所であった。その船岡山に神社を移すことになり、[[1880年]]（明治13年）9月、社殿が竣工して東京より[[遷宮|遷座]]した。なお、この時は現在表参道を少し登ったところにある「大平和敬神」の石碑が建っている場所に本殿があった。[[1881年]]（明治14年）に[[織田信忠]]が[[合祀]]されている。[[1910年]]（明治43年）に社殿が現在の山頂部分に移建された。 戦後、近代社格制度の廃止に伴い[[1948年]]（[[昭和]]23年）に[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列されたが、[[2019年]]（[[令和]]元年）に神社本庁から離脱した。 祭神・織田信長の業績にちなみ、国家安泰・難局突破・大願成就の神社とされる。高台にある境内からは特に[[大文字山]]・[[比叡山]]方面の眺望が良い。また、大鳥居は京都府内最大の白木の鳥居である。 == 祭神 == * 主祭神 - [[織田信長]]公 * 配祀神 - [[織田信忠]]卿 == 境内 == * 本殿（国[[登録有形文化財]]） - [[1880年]]（[[明治]]13年）9月建立。[[1910年]]（明治43年）に現在地に移築される。 * 渡廊（国登録有形文化財） * 透塀（国登録有形文化財） * 神門（祝詞舎、国登録有形文化財） * 拝殿（国登録有形文化財） - 内側には織田信長36功臣のうち18功臣の額を飾っている。 * 神饌所（国登録有形文化財） * 手水舎（国登録有形文化財） * 祭器庫（国登録有形文化財） * 社務所（国登録有形文化財） * 貴賓館（国登録有形文化財） * 船岡妙見社 - 祭神：[[玄武]]大神（[[妙見菩薩]]）。[[船岡山]]の地の神。 * 義照[[稲荷神社]] - 祭神：[[ウカノミタマ|宇迦御霊大神]]、[[国之常立神|国床立大神]]、[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]] * 稲荷命婦元宮 - 祭神：船岡山の霊狐。[[伏見稲荷大社]]命婦社の親神。 == 文化財 == [[画像:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[画像:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|祝詞舎]] === 重要文化財 === * 刀 無銘（[[義元左文字|宗三左文字]]） * 紺糸威胴丸（附 保呂串付受筒、采配、鞭、櫃） * [[信長公記]] [[太田牛一]]自筆本 15冊 第十五冊末[[元禄]]十二年季夏[[近衛家熙]]跋 === 国指定史跡 === * 船岡山 - 当社境内を含む山全体が史跡となっている。 === 国登録有形文化財 === * 本殿 * 拝殿 * 祝詞舎 * 渡廊 * 透塀 * 神饌所 * 祭器庫 * 手水舎 * 貴賓館 * 社務所 * 大鳥居 == 祭事 == [[永禄]]11年（[[1568年]]）の信長上洛の日に因み、10月19日に船岡大祭が行われる。 == 勧請社 == 各地に勧請社がある。 [[岐阜県]][[岐阜市]]若宮町の[[橿森神社]]では、信長が美園で開いた[[楽市・楽座]]の[[市神]]が橿森神社の御神木に祀られたという伝えがあり、明治になって境内に建勲神社を勧請した。 == アクセス == * 表参道へは[[京都市営バス|京都市バス]]「建勲（けんくん）神社前」（[[京都市営バス西賀茂営業所#1号系統|1]]・[[京都市営バス西賀茂営業所#12号系統|12]]・[[京都市営バス烏丸営業所#204号系統|204]]・[[京都市営バス九条営業所#205・快速205号系統|205]]・[[京都市営バス烏丸営業所#206号系統|206]]・[[京都市営バス烏丸営業所#北8号系統|北8]]・[[京都市営バス梅津営業所#M1・特M1号系統|M1]]系統）バス停から徒歩。 * 表参道は急な石段が続くため、それを避けるなら京都市バス船岡山バス停から徒歩で北参道。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 二六興信所編纂　山田米吉編『勤王事蹟別格官幣社精史』42〜45頁　二六興信所　1935年（国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション）] * 『織田藩と天童』 [[天童市立旧東村山郡役所資料館]]編集（[[1993年]]（[[平成]]5年）3月31日） == 関連図書 == * [[白井永二]]・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、[[1979年]]（[[昭和]]54年）、211頁 == 関連項目 == * [[松原久子]] - 建勲神社を生家とする著作家。 * [[船岡山城]] * [[劔神社]] - 織田家の[[氏神]]。 == 外部リンク == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=建勲神社}} - 公式ホームページ {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たけいさおしんしや}} [[Category:建勲神社|!]] [[Category:京都市の重要文化財]] [[Category:京都府の登録有形文化財]] [[Category:京都市の神社]] [[Category:別格官幣社]] [[Category:京都市北区の歴史]] [[Category:織田信長|社たけいさおしんしや]] [[Category:1880年竣工の建築物]] [[Category:京都市北区の建築物]] [[Category:19世紀の日本の設立]]

{{Otheruses|京都市北区にある建勲神社|山形県天童市にある建勲神社|建勲神社 (天童市)|兵庫県丹波市にある建勲神社|建勲神社 (丹波市)}} {{神社 |名称=建勲神社 |画像=[[画像:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
拝殿
{{Infobox mapframe|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-width=270|type=point}} |所在地=[[京都府]][[京都市]][[北区 (京都市)|北区]]紫野北舟岡町49 | 緯度度 = 35 | 緯度分 = 2 | 緯度秒 = 19 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 135 |経度分 = 44 | 経度秒 = 35 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP |祭神=[[織田信長]]公、[[織田信忠]]卿 |社格=旧[[別格官幣社]]
元・[[別表神社]] |創建=[[1870年]]（[[明治]]3年） |本殿= 一間社[[流造]] |例祭=7月1日 |地図=Japan Kyoto city }} '''建勲神社'''（たけいさおじんじゃ）は、[[京都市]][[北区 (京都市)|北区]]紫野北舟岡町にある[[神社]]。旧社格は[[別格官幣社]]で元・[[別表神社]]。[[船岡山]]の中腹にある。[[織田信長]]を主祭神とし、子の[[織田信忠]]を配祀する。正しくは「たけいさおじんじゃ」と読むが、一般には「けんくんじんじゃ」と呼ばれ、「建勲（けんくん）さん」と通称される同神社最寄りの京都市バスのバス停の「建勲神社前」は「けんくんじんじゃまえ」と読む。。旧称'''健織田社'''（たけしおりたのやしろ）、'''建勲社'''（たけいさおしゃ）。[[江戸時代]]後期から[[明治]]時代初期に流行した[[藩祖]]を祀った神社のひとつである。 == 歴史 == [[1869年]]（[[明治]]2年）[[11月8日 (旧暦)|11月8日]]、「日本が外国に侵略されなかったのは、天下統一をめざして日本を一つにまとめた信長のおかげ」として[http://zasshi.news.yahoo.co.jp/article?a=20141116-00000001-sasahi-peo　織田家第18代当主　織田家の400年の伝統を破り、息子の名に「信」をつけず〈週刊朝日 　2014年11月21日号〉]dot. 11月16日、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]を天下統一、朝儀復興などを進めた[[織田信長]]を賛えるための'''健織田社'''（たけしおりたのやしろ）の創建が[[明治天皇]]により決定された。翌[[1870年]]（明治3年）[[10月17日 (旧暦)|10月17日]]、「建勲」の神号を賜り、信長の子孫で[[天童藩]][[知藩事|知事]]・[[織田信敏]]の[[東京]]の邸内と[[織田氏|織田家]]旧領地の[[山形県]][[天童市]]に建勲社が造営された（天童市に造営された建勲社については[[建勲神社 (天童市)]]を参照のこと）。[[1875年]]（明治8年）4月24日に[[近代社格制度|別格官幣社]]に列格した。 現在地の[[船岡山]]は[[平安京]]の[[四神相応]]の[[玄武]]に位置し、完成しなかったとはいえ[[豊臣秀吉]]によって信長の廟所・[[天正寺 (京都市)|天正寺]]の境内地と定められた場所であった。その船岡山に神社を移すことになり、[[1880年]]（明治13年）9月、社殿が竣工して東京より[[遷宮|遷座]]した。なお、この時は現在表参道を少し登ったところにある「大平和敬神」の石碑が建っている場所に本殿があった。[[1881年]]（明治14年）に[[織田信忠]]が[[合祀]]されている。[[1910年]]（明治43年）に社殿が現在の山頂部分に移建された。 戦後、近代社格制度の廃止に伴い[[1948年]]（[[昭和]]23年）に[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列されたが、[[2019年]]（[[令和]]元年）に神社本庁から離脱した。 祭神・織田信長の業績にちなみ、国家安泰・難局突破・大願成就の神社とされる。高台にある境内からは特に[[大文字山]]・[[比叡山]]方面の眺望が良い。また、大鳥居は京都府内最大の白木の鳥居である。 == 祭神 == * 主祭神 - [[織田信長]]公 * 配祀神 - [[織田信忠]]卿 == 境内 == * 本殿（国[[登録有形文化財]]） - [[1880年]]（[[明治]]13年）9月建立。[[1910年]]（明治43年）に現在地に移築される。 * 渡廊（国登録有形文化財） * 透塀（国登録有形文化財） * 神門（祝詞舎、国登録有形文化財） * 拝殿（国登録有形文化財） - 内側には織田信長36功臣のうち18功臣の額を飾っている。 * 神饌所（国登録有形文化財） * 手水舎（国登録有形文化財） * 祭器庫（国登録有形文化財） * 社務所（国登録有形文化財） * 貴賓館（国登録有形文化財） * 船岡妙見社 - 祭神：[[玄武]]大神（[[妙見菩薩]]）。[[船岡山]]の地の神。 * 義照[[稲荷神社]] - 祭神：[[ウカノミタマ|宇迦御霊大神]]、[[国之常立神|国床立大神]]、[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]] * 稲荷命婦元宮 - 祭神：船岡山の霊狐。[[伏見稲荷大社]]命婦社の親神。 == 文化財 == [[画像:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[画像:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|祝詞舎]] === 重要文化財 === * 刀 無銘（[[義元左文字|宗三左文字]]） * 紺糸威胴丸（附 保呂串付受筒、采配、鞭、櫃） * [[信長公記]] [[太田牛一]]自筆本 15冊 第十五冊末[[元禄]]十二年季夏[[近衛家熙]]跋 === 国指定史跡 === * 船岡山 - 当社境内を含む山全体が史跡となっている。 === 国登録有形文化財 === * 本殿 * 拝殿 * 祝詞舎 * 渡廊 * 透塀 * 神饌所 * 祭器庫 * 手水舎 * 貴賓館 * 社務所 * 大鳥居 == 祭事 == [[永禄]]11年（[[1568年]]）の信長上洛の日に因み、10月19日に船岡大祭が行われる。 == 勧請社 == 各地に勧請社がある。 [[岐阜県]][[岐阜市]]若宮町の[[橿森神社]]では、信長が美園で開いた[[楽市・楽座]]の[[市神]]が橿森神社の御神木に祀られたという伝えがあり、明治になって境内に建勲神社を勧請した。 == アクセス == * 表参道へは[[京都市営バス|京都市バス]]「建勲（けんくん）神社前」（[[京都市営バス西賀茂営業所#1号系統|1]]・[[京都市営バス西賀茂営業所#12号系統|12]]・[[京都市営バス烏丸営業所#204号系統|204]]・[[京都市営バス九条営業所#205・快速205号系統|205]]・[[京都市営バス烏丸営業所#206号系統|206]]・[[京都市営バス烏丸営業所#北8号系統|北8]]・[[京都市営バス梅津営業所#M1・特M1号系統|M1]]系統）バス停から徒歩。 * 表参道は急な石段が続くため、それを避けるなら京都市バス船岡山バス停から徒歩で北参道。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 二六興信所編纂　山田米吉編『勤王事蹟別格官幣社精史』42〜45頁　二六興信所　1935年（国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション）] * 『織田藩と天童』 [[天童市立旧東村山郡役所資料館]]編集（[[1993年]]（[[平成]]5年）3月31日） == 関連図書 == * [[白井永二]]・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、[[1979年]]（[[昭和]]54年）、211頁 == 関連項目 == * [[松原久子]] - 建勲神社を生家とする著作家。 * [[船岡山城]] * [[劔神社]] - 織田家の[[氏神]]。 == 外部リンク == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=建勲神社}} - 公式ホームページ {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たけいさおしんしや}} [[Category:建勲神社|!]] [[Category:京都市の重要文化財]] [[Category:京都府の登録有形文化財]] [[Category:京都市の神社]] [[Category:別格官幣社]] [[Category:京都市北区の歴史]] [[Category:織田信長|社たけいさおしんしや]] [[Category:1880年竣工の建築物]] [[Category:京都市北区の建築物]] [[Category:19世紀の日本の設立]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Kenkun Shrine | image = Kagura-den at Kenkun Jinja Spring 2010.JPG | caption = The [[Kagura]]den at Kenkun shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|19|N|135|44|35|E|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Oda Nobunaga]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Meiji]] | established = 1869 | date_destroyed = | location = Kyōto-fu
Kyōto-shi
Kita-ku
Murasakino
Kitafunaoka-machi 49 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kenkun Shrine'''|建勲神社,|''Kenkun-jinja''}} also known as '''Takeisao Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. One of the four shrines that protect Kyoto in the four cardinal directions, it protects Kyoto from the north. [[Oda Nobunaga]], a ''[[daimyō]]'' and key figure in the unification of Japan during the late 16th century, is [[Imperial cult|deified]] and buried inside.{{Cite book| last=Dougill |first=John | title =Kyoto: a cultural history | publisher =Oxford University Press | year =2006 | pages =111 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=ggmbp2pv8toC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA111 | isbn =978-0-19-530138-0 }} ==Overview== [[zh:建勳神社]][[ja:建勲神社]] {{Otheruses|Kenkun Shrine in Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kenkun Shrine in Tendo, Yamagata|Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)|Kenkun Shrine in Tamba, Hyogo|Kenkun Shrine (Tamba)}} {{神社 |名称= Kenkun Shrine |画像=[[File:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |所在地=[[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], Murasakino Kitafunaokayama-cho 49 |緯度度=35 |緯度分=2 |緯度秒=19 |N(北緯)及びS(南緯)=N |経度度=135 |経度分=44 |経度秒=35 |E(東経)及びW(西経)=E |地図国コード=JP |祭神=[[Oda Nobunaga]] and [[Oda Nobutada]] |社格=Former [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]]
Former [[Bekkaku Shrine]] |創建=1870 (Meiji 3) |本殿= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |例祭=July 1 |地図=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. The former shrine rank was [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] and it was previously a [[Bekkaku Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is [[Oda Nobunaga]], with his son [[Oda Nobutada]] also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by [[Emperor Meiji]] to honor [[Oda Nobunaga]], who aimed for the unification of Japan during the [[Sengoku period]] and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[genbu]] area of the [[Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Bekkaku Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - Deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - Deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == Festivals == The Funaoka Grand Festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == Related Topics == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たけいさおしんしや}} [[Category:Kenkun Shrines|!]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Kyoto City]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]][[en:Kenkun Shrine]][[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[Category:History of Kita-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Oda Nobunaga|Shrines dedicated to Oda Nobunaga]] [[Category:Buildings completed in 1880]] [[Category:19th-century establishments in Japan]] ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[Four Symbols]] **[[Black Tortoise]] *[[Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1869]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in 1869]] [[Category:1869 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Former Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]][[en:Kenkun Shrine]][[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]] [[en:Kenkun Shrine]]

Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Bekkaku Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - Deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - Deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == Festivals == The Funaoka Grand Festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == Related Topics == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たけいさおしんしや}} [[Category:Kenkun Shrines|!]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Kyoto City]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[en:Kenkun Shrine]][[Category:History of Kita-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Oda Nobunaga|Shrines dedicated to Oda Nobunaga]] [[Category:Buildings completed in 1880]] [[Category:19th-century establishments in Japan]] ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[Four Symbols]] **[[Black Tortoise]] *[[Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1869]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in 1869]] [[Category:1869 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Former Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[en:Kenkun Shrine]][[en:Kenkun Shrine]]

Kitafunaoka-machi 49 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kenkun Shrine'''|建勲神社,|''Kenkun-jinja''}} also known as '''Takeisao Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. One of the four shrines that protect Kyoto in the four cardinal directions, it protects Kyoto from the north. [[Oda Nobunaga]], a ''[[daimyō]]'' and key figure in the unification of Japan during the late 16th century, is [[Imperial cult|deified]] and buried inside.{{Cite book| last=Dougill |first=John | title =Kyoto: a cultural history | publisher =Oxford University Press | year =2006 | pages =111 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=ggmbp2pv8toC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA111 | isbn =978-0-19-530138-0 }} ==Overview== [[zh:建勳神社]][[ja:建勲神社]] {{Otheruses|Kenkun Shrine in Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kenkun Shrine in Tendo, Yamagata|Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)|Kenkun Shrine in Tamba, Hyogo|Kenkun Shrine (Tamba)}} {{神社 |名称= Kenkun Shrine |画像=[[File:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |所在地=[[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], Murasakino Kitafunaokayama-cho 49 |緯度度=35 |緯度分=2 |緯度秒=19 |N(北緯)及びS(南緯)=N |経度度=135 |経度分=44 |経度秒=35 |E(東経)及びW(西経)=E |地図国コード=JP |祭神=[[Oda Nobunaga]] and [[Oda Nobutada]] |社格=Former [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]]
Former [[Bekkaku Shrine]] |創建=1870 (Meiji 3) |本殿= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |例祭=July 1 |地図=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. The former shrine rank was [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] and it was previously a [[Bekkaku Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is [[Oda Nobunaga]], with his son [[Oda Nobutada]] also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by [[Emperor Meiji]] to honor [[Oda Nobunaga]], who aimed for the unification of Japan during the [[Sengoku period]] and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[genbu]] area of the [[Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Bekkaku Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - Deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - Deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == Festivals == The Funaoka Grand Festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == Related Topics == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たけいさおしんしや}} [[Category:Kenkun Shrines|!]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Kyoto City]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kenkun Shrine}}[[category:original pages|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[en:Kenkun Shrine]][[Category:History of Kita-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Oda Nobunaga|Shrines dedicated to Oda Nobunaga]] [[Category:Buildings completed in 1880]] [[Category:19th-century establishments in Japan]] ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[Four Symbols]] **[[Black Tortoise]] *[[Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1869]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in 1869]] [[Category:1869 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Former Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kenkun Shrine}}[[category:original pages]] [[en:Kenkun Shrine]][[en:Kenkun Shrine]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Kenkun Shrine | image = Kagura-den at Kenkun Jinja Spring 2010.JPG | caption = The [[Kagura]]den at Kenkun shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|19|N|135|44|35|E|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Oda Nobunaga]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Meiji]] | established = 1869 | date_destroyed = | location = Kyōto-fu
Kyōto-shi
Kita-ku
Murasakino
Kitafunaoka-machi 49 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kenkun Shrine'''|建勲神社,|''Kenkun-jinja''}} also known as '''Takeisao Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. One of the four shrines that protect Kyoto in the four cardinal directions, it protects Kyoto from the north. [[Oda Nobunaga]], a ''[[daimyō]]'' and key figure in the unification of Japan during the late 16th century, is [[Imperial cult|deified]] and buried inside.{{Cite book| last=Dougill |first=John | title =Kyoto: a cultural history | publisher =Oxford University Press | year =2006 | pages =111 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=ggmbp2pv8toC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA111 | isbn =978-0-19-530138-0 }} ==Overview== [[zh:建勳神社]][[ja:建勲神社]] {{Otheruses|Kenkun Shrine in Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kenkun Shrine in Tendo, Yamagata|Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)|Kenkun Shrine in Tamba, Hyogo|Kenkun Shrine (Tamba)}} {{神社 |名称= Kenkun Shrine |画像=[[File:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |所在地=[[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], Murasakino Kitafunaokayama-cho 49 |緯度度=35 |緯度分=2 |緯度秒=19 |N(北緯)及びS(南緯)=N |経度度=135 |経度分=44 |経度秒=35 |E(東経)及びW(西経)=E |地図国コード=JP |祭神=[[Oda Nobunaga]] and [[Oda Nobutada]] |社格=Former [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]]
Former [[Bekkaku Shrine]] |創建=1870 (Meiji 3) |本殿= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |例祭=July 1 |地図=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. The former shrine rank was [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] and it was previously a [[Bekkaku Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is [[Oda Nobunaga]], with his son [[Oda Nobutada]] also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by [[Emperor Meiji]] to honor [[Oda Nobunaga]], who aimed for the unification of Japan during the [[Sengoku period]] and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[genbu]] area of the [[Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Bekkaku Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - Deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - Deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == Festivals == The Funaoka Grand Festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == Related Topics == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たけいさおしんしや}} [[Category:Kenkun Shrines|!]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Kyoto City]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[simple:Kenkun Shrine]][[category:original pages|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Kenkun Shrine]][[category:original pages|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kenkun Shrine}}[[category:original pages|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[en:Kenkun Shrine]][[Category:History of Kita-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Oda Nobunaga|Shrines dedicated to Oda Nobunaga]] [[Category:Buildings completed in 1880]] [[Category:19th-century establishments in Japan]] ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[Four Symbols]] **[[Black Tortoise]] *[[Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1869]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in 1869]] [[Category:1869 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Former Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:original pages]][[simple:Kenkun Shrine]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Kenkun Shrine]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kenkun Shrine}}[[category:original pages]] [[en:Kenkun Shrine]][[en:Kenkun Shrine]]

Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - Deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - Deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == Festivals == The Funaoka Grand Festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == Related Topics == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たけいさおしんしや}} [[Category:Kenkun Shrines|!]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Kyoto City]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[simple:Kenkun Shrine]][[category:original pages|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Kenkun Shrine]][[category:original pages|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kenkun Shrine}}[[category:original pages|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[en:Kenkun Shrine]][[Category:History of Kita-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Oda Nobunaga|Shrines dedicated to Oda Nobunaga]] [[Category:Buildings completed in 1880]] [[Category:19th-century establishments in Japan]] ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[Four Symbols]] **[[Black Tortoise]] *[[Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1869]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in 1869]] [[Category:1869 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Former Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:original pages]][[simple:Kenkun Shrine]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Kenkun Shrine]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kenkun Shrine}}[[category:original pages]] [[fr:Kenkun-jinja]] [[en:Kenkun Shrine]][[en:Kenkun Shrine]]

Kita-ku
Murasakino
Kitafunaoka-machi 49 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kenkun Shrine'''|建勲神社,|''Kenkun-jinja''}} also known as '''Takeisao Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. One of the four shrines that protect Kyoto in the four cardinal directions, it protects Kyoto from the north. [[Oda Nobunaga]], a ''[[daimyō]]'' and key figure in the unification of Japan during the late 16th century, is [[Imperial cult|deified]] and buried inside.{{Cite book| last=Dougill |first=John | title =Kyoto: a cultural history | publisher =Oxford University Press | year =2006 | pages =111 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=ggmbp2pv8toC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA111 | isbn =978-0-19-530138-0 }} ==Overview== [[zh:建勳神社]][[ja:建勲神社]] {{Otheruses|Kenkun Shrine in Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kenkun Shrine in Tendo, Yamagata|Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)|Kenkun Shrine in Tamba, Hyogo|Kenkun Shrine (Tamba)}} {{神社 |名称= Kenkun Shrine |画像=[[File:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |所在地=[[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], Murasakino Kitafunaokayama-cho 49 |緯度度=35 |緯度分=2 |緯度秒=19 |N(北緯)及びS(南緯)=N |経度度=135 |経度分=44 |経度秒=35 |E(東経)及びW(西経)=E |地図国コード=JP |祭神=[[Oda Nobunaga]] and [[Oda Nobutada]] |社格=Former [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]]
Former [[Bekkaku Shrine]] |創建=1870 (Meiji 3) |本殿= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |例祭=July 1 |地図=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. The former shrine rank was [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] and it was previously a [[Bekkaku Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is [[Oda Nobunaga]], with his son [[Oda Nobutada]] also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by [[Emperor Meiji]] to honor [[Oda Nobunaga]], who aimed for the unification of Japan during the [[Sengoku period]] and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[genbu]] area of the [[Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Bekkaku Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == Festivals == The Funaoka Grand Festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == Related Topics == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たけいさおしんしや}} [[Category:Kenkun Shrines|!]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Kyoto City]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[simple:Kenkun Shrine]][[category:original pages|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Kenkun Shrine]][[category:original pages|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kenkun Shrine}}[[category:original pages|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[en:Kenkun Shrine]][[Category:History of Kita-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Oda Nobunaga|Shrines dedicated to Oda Nobunaga]] [[Category:Buildings completed in 1880]] [[Category:19th-century establishments in Japan]] ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[Four Symbols]] **[[Black Tortoise]] *[[Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1869]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in 1869]] [[Category:1869 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Former Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:original pages]][[simple:Kenkun Shrine]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Kenkun Shrine]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kenkun Shrine}}[[category:original pages]] [[fr:Kenkun-jinja]] [[en:Kenkun Shrine]][[en:Kenkun Shrine]]

To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == Related Topics == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たけいさおしんしや}} [[Category:Kenkun Shrines|!]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Kyoto City]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[simple:Kenkun Shrine]][[category:original pages|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Kenkun Shrine]][[category:original pages|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kenkun Shrine}}[[category:original pages|Bekkaku Shrines]] [[en:Kenkun Shrine]][[Category:History of Kita-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Oda Nobunaga|Shrines dedicated to Oda Nobunaga]] [[Category:Buildings completed in 1880]] [[Category:19th-century establishments in Japan]] ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568.
