The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[Four Symbols]] **[[Black Tortoise]] *[[Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|建勲神社|version=102011400|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kenkun Shrines|!]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Kyoto City]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Kita-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Oda Nobunaga|Shrines dedicated to Oda Nobunaga]] [[Category:Buildings completed in 1880]] [[Category:19th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1869]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in 1869]] [[Category:1869 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Former Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo_shrines_without_templates]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:shinto shrines]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Kenkun Shrine]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] }} [[simple:Kenkun Shrine]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Kenkun Shrine]] [[sn:Kenkun Shrine]] [[zh:建勳神社]] [[ja:建勲神社]] [[en:Kenkun Shrine]] [[fr:Kenkun-jinja]] [[ja:Kenkun Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in 1880]] [[Category:京都市の重要文化財]] [[Category:京都市北区の建築物]] [[Category:京都市北区の歴史]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kenkun Shrine | image = Kagura-den at Kenkun Jinja Spring 2010.JPG | caption = The [[Kagura]]den at Kenkun shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|19|N|135|44|35|E|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Oda Nobunaga]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Meiji]] | established = 1869 | date_destroyed = | location = Kyōto-fu
Kyōto-shi
Kita-ku
Murasakino
Kitafunaoka-machi 49 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kenkun Shrine'''|建勲神社,|''Kenkun-jinja''}} also known as '''Takeisao Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. One of the four shrines that protect Kyoto in the four cardinal directions, it protects Kyoto from the north. [[Oda Nobunaga]], a ''[[daimyō]]'' and key figure in the unification of Japan during the late 16th century, is [[Imperial cult|deified]] and buried inside.{{Cite book| last=Dougill |first=John | title =Kyoto: a cultural history | publisher =Oxford University Press | year =2006 | pages =111 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=ggmbp2pv8toC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA111 | isbn =978-0-19-530138-0 }} ==Overview== {{Otheruses|Kenkun Shrine in Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kenkun Shrine in Tendo, Yamagata|Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)|Kenkun Shrine in Tamba, Hyōgo|Kenkun Shrine (Tamba)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kenkun Shrine |image=[[File:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |location=[[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], Murasakino Kitafunaokayama-cho 49 |緯度度=35 |緯度分=2 |緯度秒=19 |N(北緯)及びS(南緯)=N |経度度=135 |経度分=44 |経度秒=35 |E(東経)及びW(西経)=E |map国コード=JP |deity=[[Oda Nobunaga]] and [[Oda Nobutada]] |Rank=Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
Former [[Beppyo Shrine]] |established=1870 (Meiji 3) |main hall= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |festival=July 1 |map=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. The former shrine Rank was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and it was previously a [[Beppyo Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is [[Oda Nobunaga]], with his son [[Oda Nobutada]] also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by [[Emperor Meiji]] to honor [[Oda Nobunaga]], who aimed for the unification of Japan during the [[Sengoku period]] and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[genbu]] area of the [[Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine Ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Beppyo Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[Four Symbols]] **[[Black Tortoise]] *[[Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|建勲神社|version=102011400|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kenkun Shrines|!]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Kyoto City]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Kita-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Oda Nobunaga|Shrines dedicated to Oda Nobunaga]] [[Category:Buildings completed in 1880]] [[Category:19th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1869]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in 1869]] [[Category:1869 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Former Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo_shrines_without_templates]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:shinto shrines]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Kenkun Shrine]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] }} [[simple:Kenkun Shrine]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Kenkun Shrine]] [[sn:Kenkun Shrine]] [[zh:建勳神社]] [[ja:建勲神社]] [[en:Kenkun Shrine]] [[fr:Kenkun-jinja]] [[ja:Kenkun Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in 1880]] [[Category:京都市の重要文化財]] [[Category:京都市北区の建築物]] [[Category:京都市北区の歴史]] [[Category:京都府の登録有形文化財]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kenkun Shrine | image = Kagura-den at Kenkun Jinja Spring 2010.JPG | caption = The [[Kagura]]den at Kenkun shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|19|N|135|44|35|E|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Oda Nobunaga]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Meiji]] | established = 1869 | date_destroyed = | location = Kyōto-fu
Kyōto-shi
Kita-ku
Murasakino
Kitafunaoka-machi 49 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kenkun Shrine'''|建勲神社,|''Kenkun-jinja''}} also known as '''Takeisao Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. One of the four shrines that protect Kyoto in the four cardinal directions, it protects Kyoto from the north. [[Oda Nobunaga]], a ''[[daimyō]]'' and key figure in the unification of Japan during the late 16th century, is [[Imperial cult|deified]] and buried inside.{{Cite book| last=Dougill |first=John | title =Kyoto: a cultural history | publisher =Oxford University Press | year =2006 | pages =111 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=ggmbp2pv8toC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA111 | isbn =978-0-19-530138-0 }} ==Overview== {{Otheruses|Kenkun Shrine in Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kenkun Shrine in Tendo, Yamagata|Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)|Kenkun Shrine in Tamba, Hyōgo|Kenkun Shrine (Tamba)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kenkun Shrine |image=[[File:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |location=[[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], Murasakino Kitafunaokayama-cho 49 |緯度度=35 |緯度分=2 |緯度秒=19 |N(北緯)及びS(南緯)=N |経度度=135 |経度分=44 |経度秒=35 |E(東経)及びW(西経)=E |map国コード=JP |deity=[[Oda Nobunaga]] and [[Oda Nobutada]] |Rank=Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
Former [[Beppyo Shrine]] |established=1870 (Meiji 3) |main hall= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |festival=July 1 |map=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. The former shrine Rank was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and it was previously a [[Beppyo Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is [[Oda Nobunaga]], with his son [[Oda Nobutada]] also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by [[Emperor Meiji]] to honor [[Oda Nobunaga]], who aimed for the unification of Japan during the [[Sengoku period]] and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[genbu]] area of the [[Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine Ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Beppyo Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[Four Symbols]] **[[Black Tortoise]] *[[Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|建勲神社|version=102011400|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kenkun Shrines|!]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Kyoto City]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Kita-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Oda Nobunaga|Shrines dedicated to Oda Nobunaga]] [[Category:Buildings completed in 1880]] [[Category:19th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1869]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in 1869]] [[Category:1869 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Former Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo_shrines_without_templates]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:shinto shrines]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Kenkun Shrine]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] }} [[simple:Kenkun Shrine]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Kenkun Shrine]] [[sn:Kenkun Shrine]] [[zh:建勳神社]] [[ja:建勲神社]] [[en:Kenkun Shrine]] [[fr:Kenkun-jinja]] [[ja:Kenkun Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in 1880]] [[Category:京都市の重要文化財]] [[Category:京都市北区の建築物]] [[Category:京都市北区の歴史]] [[Category:京都府の登録有形文化財]] [[Category:建勲神社]] [[Category:織田信長]]

Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn Kita-ku
Murasakino
Kitafunaoka-machi 49 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kenkun Shrine'''|建勲神社,|''Kenkun-jinja''}} also known as '''Takeisao Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. One of the four shrines that protect Kyoto in the four cardinal directions, it protects Kyoto from the north. [[Oda Nobunaga]], a ''[[daimyō]]'' and key figure in the unification of Japan during the late 16th century, is [[Imperial cult|deified]] and buried inside.{{Cite book| last=Dougill |first=John | title =Kyoto: a cultural history | publisher =Oxford University Press | year =2006 | pages =111 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=ggmbp2pv8toC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA111 | isbn =978-0-19-530138-0 }} ==Overview== {{Otheruses|Kenkun Shrine in Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kenkun Shrine in Tendo, Yamagata|Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)|Kenkun Shrine in Tamba, Hyōgo|Kenkun Shrine (Tamba)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kenkun Shrine |image=[[File:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |location=[[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], Murasakino Kitafunaokayama-cho 49 |緯度度=35 |緯度分=2 |緯度秒=19 |N(北緯)及びS(南緯)=N |経度度=135 |経度分=44 |経度秒=35 |E(東経)及びW(西経)=E |map国コード=JP |deity=[[Oda Nobunaga]] and [[Oda Nobutada]] |Rank=Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
Former [[Beppyo Shrine]] |established=1870 (Meiji 3) |main hall= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |festival=July 1 |map=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. The former shrine Rank was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and it was previously a [[Beppyo Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is [[Oda Nobunaga]], with his son [[Oda Nobutada]] also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by [[Emperor Meiji]] to honor [[Oda Nobunaga]], who aimed for the unification of Japan during the [[Sengoku period]] and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[genbu]] area of the [[Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine Ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Beppyo Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kenkun Shrine | image = Kagura-den at Kenkun Jinja Spring 2010.JPG | caption = The [[Kagura]]den at Kenkun shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|19|N|135|44|35|E|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Oda Nobunaga]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Meiji]] | established = 1869 | date_destroyed = | location = Kyōto-fu
Kyōto-shi
Kita-ku
Murasakino
Kitafunaoka-machi 49 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kenkun Shrine'''|建勲神社,|''Kenkun-jinja''}} also known as '''Takeisao Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. One of the four shrines that protect Kyoto in the four cardinal directions, it protects Kyoto from the north. [[Oda Nobunaga]], a ''[[daimyō]]'' and key figure in the unification of Japan during the late 16th century, is [[Imperial cult|deified]] and buried inside.{{Cite book| last=Dougill |first=John | title =Kyoto: a cultural history | publisher =Oxford University Press | year =2006 | pages =111 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=ggmbp2pv8toC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA111 | isbn =978-0-19-530138-0 }} ==Overview== {{Otheruses|Kenkun Shrine in Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kenkun Shrine in Tendo, Yamagata|Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)|Kenkun Shrine in Tamba, Hyōgo|Kenkun Shrine (Tamba)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kenkun Shrine |image=[[File:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |location=[[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], Murasakino Kitafunaokayama-cho 49 |緯度度=35 |緯度分=2 |緯度秒=19 |N(北緯)及びS(南緯)=N |経度度=135 |経度分=44 |経度秒=35 |E(東経)及びW(西経)=E |map国コード=JP |deity=[[Oda Nobunaga]] and [[Oda Nobutada]] |Rank=Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
Former [[Beppyo Shrine]] |established=1870 (Meiji 3) |main hall= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |festival=July 1 |map=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. The former shrine Rank was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and it was previously a [[Beppyo Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is [[Oda Nobunaga]], with his son [[Oda Nobutada]] also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by [[Emperor Meiji]] to honor [[Oda Nobunaga]], who aimed for the unification of Japan during the [[Sengoku period]] and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[genbu]] area of the [[Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine Ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Beppyo Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[Four Symbols]] **[[Black Tortoise]] *[[Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|建勲神社|version=102011400|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kenkun Shrines|!]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Kyoto City]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Kita-ku, Kyoto]] On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[genbu]] area of the [[Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine Ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Beppyo Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[Four Symbols]] **[[Black Tortoise]] *[[Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Former [[Beppyo Shrine]] |established=1870 (Meiji 3) |main hall= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |festival=July 1 |map=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. The former shrine Rank was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and it was previously a [[Beppyo Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is [[Oda Nobunaga]], with his son [[Oda Nobutada]] also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by [[Emperor Meiji]] to honor [[Oda Nobunaga]], who aimed for the unification of Japan during the [[Sengoku period]] and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[genbu]] area of the [[Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine Ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Beppyo Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[Four Symbols]] **[[Black Tortoise]] *[[Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kenkun Shrine | image = Kagura-den at Kenkun Jinja Spring 2010.JPG | caption = The [[Kagura]]den at Kenkun shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|19|N|135|44|35|E|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Oda Nobunaga]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Meiji]] | established = 1869 | date_destroyed = | location = Kyōto-fu
Kyōto-shi
Kita-ku
Murasakino
Kitafunaoka-machi 49 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kenkun Shrine'''|建勲神社,|''Kenkun-jinja''}} also known as '''Takeisao Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. One of the four shrines that protect Kyoto in the four cardinal directions, it protects Kyoto from the north. [[Oda Nobunaga]], a ''[[daimyō]]'' and key figure in the unification of Japan during the late 16th century, is [[Imperial cult|deified]] and buried inside.{{Cite book| last=Dougill |first=John | title =Kyoto: a cultural history | publisher =Oxford University Press | year =2006 | pages =111 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=ggmbp2pv8toC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA111 | isbn =978-0-19-530138-0 }} ==Overview== {{Otheruses|Kenkun Shrine in Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kenkun Shrine in Tendo, Yamagata|Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)|Kenkun Shrine in Tamba, Hyōgo|Kenkun Shrine (Tamba)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kenkun Shrine |image=[[File:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |location=[[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], Murasakino Kitafunaokayama-cho 49 |緯度度=35 |緯度分=2 |緯度秒=19 |N(北緯)及びS(南緯)=N |経度度=135 |経度分=44 |経度秒=35 |E(東経)及びW(西経)=E |map国コード=JP |deity=[[Oda Nobunaga]] and [[Oda Nobutada]] |Rank=Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
Former [[Beppyo Shrine]] |established=1870 (Meiji 3) |main hall= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |festival=July 1 |map=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. The former shrine Rank was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and it was previously a [[Beppyo Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is [[Oda Nobunaga]], with his son [[Oda Nobutada]] also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by [[Emperor Meiji]] to honor [[Oda Nobunaga]], who aimed for the unification of Japan during the [[Sengoku period]] and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[genbu]] area of the [[Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine Ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Beppyo Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[Four Symbols]] **[[Black Tortoise]] *[[Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Kitafunaoka-machi 49 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kenkun Shrine'''|建勲神社,|''Kenkun-jinja''}} also known as '''Takeisao Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. One of the four shrines that protect Kyoto in the four cardinal directions, it protects Kyoto from the north. [[Oda Nobunaga]], a ''[[daimyō]]'' and key figure in the unification of Japan during the late 16th century, is [[Imperial cult|deified]] and buried inside.{{Cite book| last=Dougill |first=John | title =Kyoto: a cultural history | publisher =Oxford University Press | year =2006 | pages =111 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=ggmbp2pv8toC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA111 | isbn =978-0-19-530138-0 }} ==Overview== {{Otheruses|Kenkun Shrine in Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kenkun Shrine in Tendo, Yamagata|Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)|Kenkun Shrine in Tamba, Hyōgo|Kenkun Shrine (Tamba)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kenkun Shrine |image=[[File:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |location=[[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], Murasakino Kitafunaokayama-cho 49 |緯度度=35 |緯度分=2 |緯度秒=19 |N(北緯)及びS(南緯)=N |経度度=135 |経度分=44 |経度秒=35 |E(東経)及びW(西経)=E |map国コード=JP |deity=[[Oda Nobunaga]] and [[Oda Nobutada]] |Rank=Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
Former [[Beppyo Shrine]] |established=1870 (Meiji 3) |main hall= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |festival=July 1 |map=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto City]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. The former shrine Rank was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and it was previously a [[Beppyo Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is [[Oda Nobunaga]], with his son [[Oda Nobutada]] also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by [[Emperor Meiji]] to honor [[Oda Nobunaga]], who aimed for the unification of Japan during the [[Sengoku period]] and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[genbu]] area of the [[Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine Ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Beppyo Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * [[Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[Four Symbols]] **[[Black Tortoise]] *[[Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org
