One of the four shrines that protect Kyoto in the four cardinal directions, it protects Kyoto from the north. [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' and key figure in the unification of Japan during the late 16th century, is [[:en:Imperial cult|deified]] and buried inside.{{Cite book| last=Dougill |first=John | title =Kyoto: a cultural history | publisher =Oxford University Press | year =2006 | pages =111 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=ggmbp2pv8toC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA111 | isbn =978-0-19-530138-0 }} ==Overview== {{Otheruses|Kenkun Shrine in Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kenkun Shrine in Tendo, Yamagata|Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)|Kenkun Shrine in Tanba, Hyōgo|Kenkun Shrine (Tanba)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kenkun Shrine |image=[[File:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |location=[[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], Murasakino Kitafunaokayama-cho 49 |緯度度=35 |緯度分=2 |緯度秒=19 |N(北緯)及びS(南緯)=N |経度度=135 |経度分=44 |経度秒=35 |E(東経)及びW(西経)=E |map国コード=JP |deity=[[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Oda Nobutada|Oda Nobutada]] |Rank=Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
Former [[Beppyo Shrine]] |established=1870 (Meiji 3) |main hall= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |festival=July 1 |map=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. The former shrine Rank was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and it was previously a [[Beppyo Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], with his son [[:en:Oda Nobutada|Oda Nobutada]] also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] to honor [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], who aimed for the unification of Japan during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[:en:Oda Nobutaka|Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[:en:Tendo Domain|Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[:en:Genbu|Genbu]] area of the [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine Ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Beppyo Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[:en:Oda Nobutada|Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[:en:Konoe Iehiro|Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[:en:Kashimori Shrine|Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * [[:en:Hisako Matsubara|Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[:en:Festival|Festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto|List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[:en:Four Symbols|Four Symbols]] **[[:en:Black Tortoise|Black Tortoise]] *[[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Official website] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|建勲神社|version=102011400|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Kita-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Oda Nobunaga|Shrines dedicated to Oda One of the four shrines that protect Kyoto in the four cardinal directions, it protects Kyoto from the north. [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' and key figure in the unification of Japan during the late 16th century, is [[:en:Imperial cult|deified]] and buried inside.{{Cite book| last=Dougill |first=John | title =Kyoto: a cultural history | publisher =Oxford University Press | year =2006 | pages =111 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=ggmbp2pv8toC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA111 | isbn =978-0-19-530138-0 }} ==Overview== {{Otheruses|Kenkun Shrine in Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kenkun Shrine in Tendo, Yamagata|Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)|Kenkun Shrine in Tanba, Hyōgo|Kenkun Shrine (Tanba)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kenkun Shrine |image=[[File:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |location=[[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], Murasakino Kitafunaokayama-cho 49 |緯度度=35 |緯度分=2 |緯度秒=19 |N(北緯)及びS(南緯)=N |経度度=135 |経度分=44 |経度秒=35 |E(東経)及びW(西経)=E |map国コード=JP |deity=[[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Oda Nobutada|Oda Nobutada]] |Rank=Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
Former [[Beppyo Shrine]] |established=1870 (Meiji 3) |main hall= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |festival=July 1 |map=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. The former shrine Rank was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and it was previously a [[Beppyo Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], with his son [[:en:Oda Nobutada|Oda Nobutada]] also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] to honor [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], who aimed for the unification of Japan during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[:en:Oda Nobutaka|Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[:en:Tendo Domain|Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[:en:Genbu|Genbu]] area of the [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine Ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Beppyo Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[:en:Oda Nobutada|Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[:en:Konoe Iehiro|Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[:en:Kashimori Shrine|Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * [[:en:Hisako Matsubara|Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[:en:Festival|Festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto|List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[:en:Four Symbols|Four Symbols]] **[[:en:Black Tortoise|Black Tortoise]] *[[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Official website] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|建勲神社|version=102011400|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Kita-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Oda Nobunaga|Shrines dedicated to Oda Kitafunaoka-machi 49 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Kita-ku|WD=Q1154970}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kenkun Shrine'''|建勲神社,|''Kenkun-jinja''}} also known as '''Takeisao Shrine''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. One of the four shrines that protect Kyoto in the four cardinal directions, it protects Kyoto from the north. [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' and key figure in the unification of Japan during the late 16th century, is [[:en:Imperial cult|deified]] and buried inside.{{Cite book| last=Dougill |first=John | title =Kyoto: a cultural history | publisher =Oxford University Press | year =2006 | pages =111 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=ggmbp2pv8toC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA111 | isbn =978-0-19-530138-0 }} ==Overview== {{Otheruses|Kenkun Shrine in Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kenkun Shrine in Tendo, Yamagata|Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)|Kenkun Shrine in Tanba, Hyōgo|Kenkun Shrine (Tanba)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kenkun Shrine |image=[[File:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |location=[[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], Murasakino Kitafunaokayama-cho 49 |緯度度=35 |緯度分=2 |緯度秒=19 |N(北緯)及びS(南緯)=N |経度度=135 |経度分=44 |経度秒=35 |E(東経)及びW(西経)=E |map国コード=JP |deity=[[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Oda Nobutada|Oda Nobutada]] |Rank=Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
Former [[Beppyo Shrine]] |established=1870 (Meiji 3) |main hall= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |festival=July 1 |map=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. The former shrine Rank was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and it was previously a [[Beppyo Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], with his son [[:en:Oda Nobutada|Oda Nobutada]] also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] to honor [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], who aimed for the unification of Japan during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[:en:Oda Nobutaka|Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[:en:Tendo Domain|Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[:en:Genbu|Genbu]] area of the [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine Ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Beppyo Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[:en:Oda Nobutada|Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[:en:Konoe Iehiro|Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[:en:Kashimori Shrine|Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * [[:en:Hisako Matsubara|Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[:en:Festival|Festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto|List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[:en:Four Symbols|Four Symbols]] **[[:en:Black Tortoise|Black Tortoise]] *[[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Official website] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|建勲神社|version=102011400|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[simple:Kenkun Shrine]] [[zh:建勳神社]] [[ja:建勲神社]] [[en:Kenkun Shrine]] On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[:en:Oda Nobutaka|Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[:en:Tendo Domain|Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[:en:Genbu|Genbu]] area of the [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[:en:Konoe Iehiro|Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. Kitafunaoka-machi 49 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Kita-ku|WD=Q1154970}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kenkun Shrine'''|建勲神社,|''Kenkun-jinja''}} also known as '''Takeisao Shrine''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. One of the four shrines that protect Kyoto in the four cardinal directions, it protects Kyoto from the north. [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' and key figure in the unification of Japan during the late 16th century, is [[:en:Imperial cult|deified]] and buried inside.{{Cite book| last=Dougill |first=John | title =Kyoto: a cultural history | publisher =Oxford University Press | year =2006 | pages =111 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=ggmbp2pv8toC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA111 | isbn =978-0-19-530138-0 }} ==Overview== {{Otheruses|Kenkun Shrine in Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kenkun Shrine in Tendo, Yamagata|Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)|Kenkun Shrine in Tanba, Hyōgo|Kenkun Shrine (Tanba)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kenkun Shrine |image=[[File:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |location=[[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], Murasakino Kitafunaokayama-cho 49 |緯度度=35 |緯度分=2 |緯度秒=19 |N(北緯)及びS(南緯)=N |経度度=135 |経度分=44 |経度秒=35 |E(東経)及びW(西経)=E |map国コード=JP |deity=[[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Oda Nobutada|Oda Nobutada]] |Rank=Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
Former [[Beppyo Shrine]] |established=1870 (Meiji 3) |main hall= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |festival=July 1 |map=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. The former shrine Rank was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and it was previously a [[Beppyo Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], with his son [[:en:Oda Nobutada|Oda Nobutada]] also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] to honor [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], who aimed for the unification of Japan during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[:en:Oda Nobutaka|Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[:en:Tendo Domain|Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[:en:Genbu|Genbu]] area of the [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine Ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Beppyo Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[:en:Oda Nobutada|Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[:en:Konoe Iehiro|Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[:en:Kashimori Shrine|Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * [[:en:Hisako Matsubara|Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[:en:Festival|Festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto|List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[:en:Four Symbols|Four Symbols]] **[[:en:Black Tortoise|Black Tortoise]] *[[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Official website] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|建勲神社|version=102011400|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[simple:Kenkun Shrine]] [[zh:建勳神社]] [[ja:建勲神社]] [[en:Kenkun Shrine]] The former shrine Rank was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and it was previously a [[Beppyo Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], with his son [[:en:Oda Nobutada|Oda Nobutada]] also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] to honor [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], who aimed for the unification of Japan during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[:en:Oda Nobutaka|Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[:en:Tendo Domain|Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[:en:Genbu|Genbu]] area of the [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[:en:Konoe Iehiro|Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[:en:Kashimori Shrine|Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * [[:en:Hisako Matsubara|Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[:en:Festival|Festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Kitafunaoka-machi 49 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Kita-ku|WD=Q1154970}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kenkun Shrine'''|建勲神社,|''Kenkun-jinja''}} also known as '''Takeisao Shrine''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. One of the four shrines that protect Kyoto in the four cardinal directions, it protects Kyoto from the north. [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' and key figure in the unification of Japan during the late 16th century, is [[:en:Imperial cult|deified]] and buried inside.{{Cite book| last=Dougill |first=John | title =Kyoto: a cultural history | publisher =Oxford University Press | year =2006 | pages =111 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=ggmbp2pv8toC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA111 | isbn =978-0-19-530138-0 }} ==Overview== {{Otheruses|Kenkun Shrine in Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kenkun Shrine in Tendo, Yamagata|Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)|Kenkun Shrine in Tanba, Hyōgo|Kenkun Shrine (Tanba)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kenkun Shrine |image=[[File:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |location=[[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], Murasakino Kitafunaokayama-cho 49 |緯度度=35 |緯度分=2 |緯度秒=19 |N(北緯)及びS(南緯)=N |経度度=135 |経度分=44 |経度秒=35 |E(東経)及びW(西経)=E |map国コード=JP |deity=[[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Oda Nobutada|Oda Nobutada]] |Rank=Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
Former [[Beppyo Shrine]] |established=1870 (Meiji 3) |main hall= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |festival=July 1 |map=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. The former shrine Rank was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and it was previously a [[Beppyo Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], with his son [[:en:Oda Nobutada|Oda Nobutada]] also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] to honor [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], who aimed for the unification of Japan during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, [[:en:Oda Nobutaka|Oda Nobutaka]], the lord of [[:en:Tendo Domain|Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the [[:en:Genbu|Genbu]] area of the [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] and was intended by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine Ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Beppyo Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] * Enshrined deity - [[:en:Oda Nobutada|Oda Nobutada]] == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by [[:en:Konoe Iehiro|Konoe Iehiro]] dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At [[:en:Kashimori Shrine|Kashimori Shrine]] in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * [[:en:Hisako Matsubara|Hisako Matsubara]] - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a [[:en:Festival|Festival]] held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto|List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *[[:en:Four Symbols|Four Symbols]] **[[:en:Black Tortoise|Black Tortoise]] *[[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Official website] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|建勲神社|version=102011400|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[simple:Kenkun Shrine]] [[zh:建勳神社]] [[ja:建勲神社]] [[en:Kenkun Shrine]] [[fr:Kenkun-jinja]] [[ja:Kenkun Shrine]] Kyōto-shi
Kita-ku
Murasakino
Kitafunaoka-machi 49 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Kita-ku|WD=Q1154970}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kenkun Shrine'''|建勲神社,|''Kenkun-jinja''}} also known as '''Takeisao Shrine''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the city of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. One of the four shrines that protect Kyoto in the four cardinal directions, it protects Kyoto from the north. {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}, a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' and key figure in the unification of Japan during the late 16th century, is {{ill|Imperial cult|en|Imperial cult|WD=Q3271557|lt=deified}} and buried inside.{{Cite book| last=Dougill |first=John | title =Kyoto: a cultural history | publisher =Oxford University Press | year =2006 | pages =111 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=ggmbp2pv8toC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA111 | isbn =978-0-19-530138-0 }} ==Overview== {{Otheruses|Kenkun Shrine in Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kenkun Shrine in Tendo, Yamagata|Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)|Kenkun Shrine in Tanba, Hyōgo|Kenkun Shrine (Tanba)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kenkun Shrine |image=[[File:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |location={{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}, [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]], {{ill|Kita-ku, Kyoto|en|Kita-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154970|lt=Kita-ku}}, Murasakino Kitafunaokayama-cho 49 |緯度度=35 |緯度分=2 |緯度秒=19 |N(北緯)及びS(南緯)=N |経度度=135 |経度分=44 |経度秒=35 |E(東経)及びW(西経)=E |map国コード=JP |deity={{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} and {{ill|Oda Nobutada|en|Oda Nobutada|WD=Q2446917|lt=Oda Nobutada}} |Rank=Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
Former [[Beppyo Shrine]] |established=1870 (Meiji 3) |main hall= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |festival=July 1 |map=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kita-ku, Kyoto|en|Kita-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154970|lt=Kita-ku}}, [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]], {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}. The former shrine Rank was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and it was previously a [[Beppyo Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}, with his son {{ill|Oda Nobutada|en|Oda Nobutada|WD=Q2446917|lt=Oda Nobutada}} also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}} to honor {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}, who aimed for the unification of Japan during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, {{ill|Oda Nobutaka|en|Oda Nobutaka|WD=Q2614349|lt=Oda Nobutaka}}, the lord of [[:en:Tendo Domain|Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture}} (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the {{ill|Genbu|en|Genbu|WD=Q5530895|lt=Genbu}} area of the {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}} and was intended by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine Ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Beppyo Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|WD=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei}}. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} * Enshrined deity - {{ill|Oda Nobutada|en|Oda Nobutada|WD=Q2446917|lt=Oda Nobutada}} == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by {{ill|Konoe Iehiro|en|Konoe Iehiro|WD=Q923579|lt=Konoe Iehiro}} dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At {{ill|Kashimori Shrine|en|Kashimori Shrine|WD=Q3193694|lt=Kashimori Shrine}} in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * {{ill|Hisako Matsubara|en|Hisako Matsubara|WD=Q273007|lt=Hisako Matsubara}} - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a {{ill|Festival|en|Festival|WD=Q132241|lt=Festival}} held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto|List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *{{ill|Four Symbols|en|Four Symbols|WD=Q1073260|lt=Four Symbols}} **[[:en:Black Tortoise|Black Tortoise]] *{{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://kenkun-jinja.org Official website] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|建勲神社|version=102011400|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[simple:Kenkun Shrine]] [[zh:建勳神社]] [[ja:建勲神社]] revision.text:
[[arz:ضريح كنكون]][[co:commons:Category:Kenkun shrine]][[en:Kenkun Shrine]][[fa:معبد کنکون]][[fr:Kenkun-jinja]][[ja:建勲神社]][[zh:建勳神社]] [[arz:ضريح كنكون]][[co:commons:Category:Kenkun shrine]][[en:Kenkun Shrine]][[fa:معبد کنکون]][[fr:Kenkun-jinja]][[ja:建勲神社]][[zh:建勳神社]] [[arz:ضريح كنكون]][[sn:commons:Category:Kenkun shrine]][[en:Kenkun Shrine]][[fa:معبد کنکون]][[fr:Kenkun-jinja]][[ja:建勲神社]][[zh:建勳神社]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767}}| name = Kenkun Shrine | image = File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg | caption = The [[Kagura]]den at Kenkun shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.03861111|N|135.74305556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | type = | deity = {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411}} | founded_by = {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}} | established = 1869 | date_destroyed = | location = Kyōto-fu
Kyōto-shi
Kita-ku
Murasakino
Kitafunaoka-machi 49 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Kita-ku|WD=Q1154970}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kenkun Shrine'''|建勲神社,|''Kenkun-jinja''}} also known as '''Takeisao Shrine''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the city of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. One of the four shrines that protect Kyoto in the four cardinal directions, it protects Kyoto from the north. {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}, a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' and key figure in the unification of Japan during the late 16th century, is {{ill|Imperial cult|en|Imperial cult|WD=Q3271557|lt=deified}} and buried inside.{{Cite book| last=Dougill |first=John | title =Kyoto: a cultural history | publisher =Oxford University Press | year =2006 | pages =111 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=ggmbp2pv8toC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA111 | isbn =978-0-19-530138-0 }} ==Overview== {{Otheruses|Kenkun Shrine in Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kenkun Shrine in Tendo, Yamagata|Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)|Kenkun Shrine in Tanba, Hyōgo|Kenkun Shrine (Tanba)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Kenkun Shrine |image=[[File:船岡山城4.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |location={{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}, [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]], {{ill|Kita-ku, Kyoto|en|Kita-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154970|lt=Kita-ku}}, Murasakino Kitafunaokayama-cho 49 |緯度度=35 |緯度分=2 |緯度秒=19 |N(北緯)及びS(南緯)=N |経度度=135 |経度分=44 |経度秒=35 |E(東経)及びW(西経)=E |map国コード=JP |deity={{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} and {{ill|Oda Nobutada|en|Oda Nobutada|WD=Q2446917|lt=Oda Nobutada}} |Rank=Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
Former [[Beppyo Shrine]] |established=1870 (Meiji 3) |main hall= Ichigan-sha Nagare-zukuri |festival=July 1 |map=Japan Kyoto city }} '''Kenkun Shrine''' (建勲神社, pronounced "Takeisao Jinja") is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kita-ku, Kyoto|en|Kita-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154970|lt=Kita-ku}}, [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]], {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}. The former shrine Rank was [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and it was previously a [[Beppyo Shrine]]. It is located on the hillside of [[Funaokayama]]. The main deity is {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}, with his son {{ill|Oda Nobutada|en|Oda Nobutada|WD=Q2446917|lt=Oda Nobutada}} also enshrined. Although the official reading is "Takeisao Jinja," it is commonly known as "Kenkun Jinja" or "Kenkun-san." == History == On November 8, 1869 (Meiji 2), the construction of "Takeisao Shrine" was decided by {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}} to honor {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}, who aimed for the unification of Japan during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} and contributed to the revival of the imperial court. On October 17, 1870 (Meiji 3), Nobunaga was granted the title "Kenkun" and shrines were built in the Tokyo residence of his descendant, {{ill|Oda Nobutaka|en|Oda Nobutaka|WD=Q2614349|lt=Oda Nobutaka}}, the lord of [[:en:Tendo Domain|Tendo Domain]], and in the old Oda clan territory in [[Tendo City]], {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture}} (see [[Kenkun Shrine (Tendo)]] for details). The shrine was designated as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] on April 24, 1875 (Meiji 8). The current location, [[Funaokayama]], is positioned in the {{ill|Genbu|en|Genbu|WD=Q5530895|lt=Genbu}} area of the {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}} and was intended by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} as the site for Nobunaga's mausoleum and the temple of [[Tenshoji]], although it was never completed. In September 1880 (Meiji 13), the shrine buildings were completed, and the shrine was relocated from Tokyo. Initially, the main shrine was located at the site where a stone monument now stands, partway up the main approach. In 1881 (Meiji 14), Nobutada was enshrined along with Nobunaga. The shrine buildings were relocated to the current mountaintop site in 1910 (Meiji 43). After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine Ranking system, the shrine was added to the list of [[Beppyo Shrine]]s of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Showa 23), but it left the association in 2019 (Reiwa 1). The shrine, honoring the achievements of Nobunaga, is dedicated to national security, overcoming difficulties, and achieving great goals. From the shrine grounds, there are excellent views of [[Mount Daimonji]] and {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|WD=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei}}. The large torii gate is the largest white wood torii in Kyoto Prefecture. == Deities == * Main deity - {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} * Enshrined deity - {{ill|Oda Nobutada|en|Oda Nobutada|WD=Q2446917|lt=Oda Nobutada}} == Grounds == * Main Shrine (National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]) - Built in September 1880 (Meiji 13) and relocated to the current site in 1910 (Meiji 43). * Connecting corridor (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Transparent wall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred gate (Noijusha, National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Worship hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Inside are plaques of 18 of Nobunaga's 36 loyal retainers. * Sacred offerings hall (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Water purification pavilion (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Sacred tool storage (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Shrine office (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Guest house (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) * Funaoka Myōken Shrine - deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by {{ill|Konoe Iehiro|en|Konoe Iehiro|WD=Q923579|lt=Konoe Iehiro}} dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At {{ill|Kashimori Shrine|en|Kashimori Shrine|WD=Q3193694|lt=Kashimori Shrine}} in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * {{ill|Hisako Matsubara|en|Hisako Matsubara|WD=Q273007|lt=Hisako Matsubara}} - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a {{ill|Festival|en|Festival|WD=Q132241|lt=Festival}} held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto|List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *{{ill|Four Symbols|en|Four Symbols|WD=Q1073260|lt=Four Symbols}} **[[:en:Black Tortoise|Black Tortoise]] *{{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External deity: Genbu (Myōken Bosatsu), the local deity of Funaokayama. * Yoshibe Inari Shrine - Deities: Uka-no-mitama-no-Ōkami, Kuni-no-tokotachi-no-Ōkami, Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami * Inari Myōbu Motomiya - deity: the spirit fox of Funaokayama, the parent deity of the Myōbu Shrine at [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]. == Cultural Properties == [[File:Domaru at Takeisao jinja 2.jpg|thumb|紺糸威胴丸]] [[File:Takeisao-jinja Honden.jpg|thumb|Noijusha]] === Important Cultural Properties === * Sword (Mumei, [Sōza Samonji]) * Dark blue thread-wrapped dōmaru armor (with attached horogushi attached case, war fan, whip, and chest) * [[Shinchokoki]] handwritten manuscript by [[Ota Gyūichi]], 15 volumes with a postscript by {{ill|Konoe Iehiro|en|Konoe Iehiro|WD=Q923579|lt=Konoe Iehiro}} dated Genroku 12 (1699). === National Historic Site === * Funaokayama - The entire mountain, including the shrine grounds, is a historic site. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine * Worship Hall * Noijusha * Connecting corridor * Transparent wall * Sacred offerings hall * Sacred tool storage * Water purification pavilion * Guest house * Shrine office * Great torii gate == festivals == The Funaoka Grand festival is held on October 19, commemorating Nobunaga's entry into Kyoto in 1568 (Eiroku 11). == Branch Shrines == There are branch shrines in various locations. At {{ill|Kashimori Shrine|en|Kashimori Shrine|WD=Q3193694|lt=Kashimori Shrine}} in [[Wakamiya-cho, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture]], a deity of the market established by Nobunaga in Misono is enshrined in a sacred tree. In the Meiji era, Kenkun Shrine was enshrined in the shrine grounds. == Access == * To the main approach, take the [[Kyoto City Bus]] to the "Kenkun Jinja-mae" stop (bus lines 1, 12, 204, 205, 206, North 8, M1) and walk. * The main approach consists of steep stone steps. To avoid these, walk from the Kyoto City Bus Funaokayama stop to the north approach. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 "History of Distinguished Shrines of Loyalists"], compiled by Niroku Koshinsho, edited by Yamada Yonekichi, pp. 42-45, Niroku Koshinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * "Oda Clan and Tendo", edited by the Tendo City Former Higashi Murayama County Office Museum (March 31, 1993 (Heisei 5)) == Related Books == * [[Eiichi Shirai]], ed., "Jinja Jiten", [[Tokyo Do Shuppan]], 1979 (Showa 54), p. 211 == See Also == * {{ill|Hisako Matsubara|en|Hisako Matsubara|WD=Q273007|lt=Hisako Matsubara}} - Writer born in the family of Kenkun Shrine. * [[Funaokayama Castle]] * [[Tsurugi Shrine]] - The Oda clan's [[ujigami]]. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Kenkun shrine}} * {{Official website|name=Kenkun Shrine}} - Official website {{神道 横}} ==Funaoka Matsuri== The Funaoka Matsuri is a {{ill|Festival|en|Festival|WD=Q132241|lt=Festival}} held every year on October 19 at Kenkun shrine commemorating the day when Nobunaga first entered Kyoto in 1568. Young boys dressed in {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} armor portray Nobunaga's army as they marched into Kyoto to take control of the government.{{Cite book| last1=Martin |first1=John H. |last2=Martin |first2=Phyllis G. | title =Kyoto: A Cultural Guide | publisher =Tuttle Publishing | year =2002 | location =Singapore | pages =339 | url =https://books.google.com/books?id=YRCWtf_d5jgC&q=kenkun+shrine&pg=PA339 | isbn =978-0-8048-3341-7 }} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto|List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] *{{ill|Four
